content.text: Naming & Descriptions

The model never reads your tool's source code. It only sees the name, description, and parameter descriptions you provide. A poorly named or described tool produces wrong calls, missed opportunities, and hallucinated arguments. Getting these right is the single highest-leverage improvement you can make to a tool-using system.

attrs.alt: Anatomy of a well-named tool showing name, description, and parameter description fields
attrs.title: Anatomy of a Well-Named Tool

content.text: Write tool names and descriptions for the model, not for humans reading your code. The model reasons about when and how to call tools entirely from the text you provide.
Tool Name Conventions
Tool names must be unique within a tool set, use only alphanumeric characters and underscores, and stay under 64 characters. Beyond these hard constraints, follow the
verb_noun
pattern: the verb describes the action, the noun describes what it acts on.

# Good names: verb + noun, clear and specific
good_names = [
    "search_products",      # find items matching criteria
    "get_user_profile",     # fetch a single user record
    "create_support_ticket",# open a new ticket
    "update_order_status",  # change status of an order
    "delete_draft",         # remove an unpublished draft
    "send_notification",    # dispatch a message
    "calculate_shipping",   # compute shipping cost
    "list_open_invoices",   # enumerate unpaid invoices
]

# Bad names: vague, no verb, or ambiguous
bad_names = [
    "data",             # what data? what action?
    "handler",          # handler for what?
    "manage_things",    # god-tool — everything and nothing
    "getUserAndOrders", # camelCase, does two things
    "tool1",            # meaningless to the model
    "process",          # verb without a noun
    "CustomerService",  # PascalCase, vague scope
]
Writing the Tool Description
A good tool description answers four questions in two to four sentences: What does it do? What does it return? When should the model use it (and when not)? Are there important limits or side effects?

# BAD descriptions — too vague for the model to act on
bad_descriptions = [
    "Gets orders",                   # what orders? from where? returns what?
    "Useful for product-related tasks",  # no action, no return value
    "Search function",               # which search? over what data?
]

# GOOD descriptions — answer all four questions
good_descriptions = [
    # search_products
    (
        "Search the product catalogue by keyword, category, or price range. "
        "Returns up to 20 products with name, price, stock level, and SKU. "
        "Use this when the user asks about available products, pricing, or inventory. "
        "Do not use this for order history or customer records."
    ),
    # send_email
    (
        "Send a transactional email to one recipient. "
        "The email is sent immediately and cannot be recalled. "
        "Use for order confirmations, password resets, and notifications — not marketing."
    ),
    # get_exchange_rate
    (
        "Fetch the current exchange rate between two ISO 4217 currency codes. "
        "Returns the mid-market rate updated every 60 seconds. "
        "Use whenever the user asks about currency conversion or international pricing."
    ),
]
Writing Parameter Descriptions

Parameter descriptions are the model's only guide for what values are valid. Include the type, allowed values or range, a concrete example when the format is non-obvious, and whether the parameter is optional.
Describing Side Effects Explicitly

Write operations must say so in plain language. If the model doesn't know a tool sends an email, deletes a record, or charges a card, it may invoke it speculatively or during testing. A single explicit sentence prevents costly mistakes.
Disambiguating Similar Tools
When two tools have overlapping functionality, the description must make the decision boundary explicit. Without guidance, the model will pick arbitrarily between them.
Testing Your Descriptions

The fastest way to validate a description is to ask the model to explain what the tool does and when it would use it — without any user prompt. If the model's explanation matches your intent, the description is clear. If it drifts, rewrite before running any real tasks.
Common Description Mistakes
Mistake
Problem
Fix
"Gets data"
No action, no return type
Describe what data, from where, and in what form
"Use when needed"
No decision rule for the model
State exact trigger: "Use when user asks about..."
No side-effect warning
Model may call speculatively
Add "This sends / deletes / charges..."
Missing parameter examples
Model guesses format, gets it wrong
Add "e.g. 'YYYY-MM-DD'" or "e.g. '+1415552671'"
Overlapping descriptions
Model picks the wrong tool
Add "Do NOT use for X — use Y instead"
No result description
Model can't plan follow-up steps
Add "Returns {field1, field2} for each result"
Iterative Description Refinement

Good descriptions rarely emerge on the first try. Treat description writing as a loop: draft, test with real prompts, observe where the model calls the wrong tool or passes wrong arguments, update the description, and re-test.
Descriptions for Enum Parameters
When a parameter has a fixed set of allowed values, list them explicitly in the JSON Schema
enum
field AND explain what each value means in the description. The model reads both.
Summary
Tool names follow
verb_noun
in snake_case — unique, under 64 chars, no spaces or special characters.
Every description answers: what does it do, what does it return, when to use it, and what side effects it has.
Parameter descriptions must state type, allowed values, format (with an example when non-obvious), and whether optional.
Write operations must name their side effect explicitly — "Sends an SMS", "Charges the card", "Deletes the record".
When two tools overlap, use "Use this for X — not for Y, use Z instead" to give the model a clear decision rule.
Test descriptions by asking the model to explain the tool in its own words — a mismatch signals a rewrite is needed.
